title: [case] Auth middleware exempts a prefix; a non-framework handler under it ships unauthenticated
body:
### Summary

A global auth middleware skips a URL prefix (e.g. everything under `/api/*`) on the assumption that the framework mounted there authenticates each handler itself. A plain **non-framework** handler registered under that same prefix — a raw view, a small utility/health endpoint, a mounted sub-app — inherits neither the middleware check (it's exempted) nor the framework's per-view auth (it isn't a framework view). It ships fully unauthenticated, and nothing errors.

### Stack

Django + DRF (generalizes to any two-layer auth: gateway/middleware + per-view framework auth)

### Failure mode

Deny-by-default is violated at the seam between two auth layers. This is a cross-layer prefix-exemption gap, distinct from auth-middleware-scope-miss (which is about encapsulation among sibling routers).

### Evidence

```python
class AuthEverywhereExceptApi:
    def __call__(self, request):
        if request.path.startswith('/api/'):  # trusts DRF to authenticate
            return self.get_response(request)
        if not request.user.is_authenticated:
            return redirect('login')
        return self.get_response(request)

def list_internal_files(request):  # NOT a DRF view, under /api
    return JsonResponse({'files': _list_share()})
urlpatterns = [path('api/check-files/', list_internal_files)]
```

### Verifier idea

Unauthenticated route-table sweep over the whole URLconf (not just the framework router): every route under the exempted prefix not in a public allowlist must return 401/403 with no credentials.

### Best target artifact

New failure card

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.

title: [case] Adding a second authentication class silently widens every IsAuthenticated-only endpoint
body:
### Summary

Authorization on many endpoints is just "is the caller authenticated?" (DRF `IsAuthenticated`), written when the only principals were staff users. Later a second authentication class is added globally — an API key for an external machine integrator. Every endpoint still guarded only by `IsAuthenticated` now silently accepts the integrator too, including admin/reference CRUD (reference tables, integration configs) it was never meant to reach. The endpoints didn't change; the meaning of the check under them did.

### Stack

Django + DRF (generalizes to any framework where a coarse "authenticated" check is used as authorization and a new authN method is added app-wide)

### Failure mode

Authentication-principal-class expansion invalidates coarse authorization. A single IsAuthenticated check conflates two principal classes once a new authN method exists; adding an authenticator re-scopes every authN-only endpoint without editing any of them.

### Evidence

```python
REST_FRAMEWORK = {"DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES": (
    "app.auth.MachineApiKeyAuth",  # added later, app-wide
    "rest_framework_simplejwt.authentication.JWTAuthentication",
    "rest_framework.authentication.SessionAuthentication",
)}

class ProductTypeViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    permission_classes = [IsAuthenticated]  # meant staff; now also the integrator
```

### Verifier idea

Principal-matrix test, run whenever a new authentication class is added: construct a principal holding only the narrow new scope, hit every registered route, assert 403 on everything outside that principal's allowlist.

### Best target artifact

New failure card

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.

title: [case] DRF has authenticators but no DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES -> fail-open (AllowAny) default
body:
### Summary

`REST_FRAMEWORK` sets `DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES` but omits `DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES`. DRF's built-in default is `AllowAny`, so every view that forgets its own `permission_classes` is public. A forgotten line fails open, not closed.

### Stack

Django + DRF (same shape in any framework whose out-of-the-box default is "no authorization")

### Failure mode

Deny-by-default not set at the framework layer; per-view permission is opt-in. This is the root cause that makes a forgotten permission_classes line a silent exposure rather than a 500.

### Evidence

```python
REST_FRAMEWORK = {
    "DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES": (...),
    # no DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES -> DRF default == AllowAny
}
```

### Verifier idea

Config assertion or Semgrep: REST_FRAMEWORK must define DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES to a non-AllowAny value; plus a route sweep so any non-allowlisted route returns 401/403. Caveat: flipping this on an existing service is a behavior change — do a before/after sweep.

### Best target artifact

Checker

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.

title: [case] Custom authenticator raises instead of returning None and reads creds from arbitrary headers
body:
### Summary

A custom DRF authentication class infers username:secret by scanning arbitrary request headers (deny-list of ~10 known names, then the first remaining header is taken as credentials), and raises AuthenticationFailed when it can't resolve a user instead of returning None. DRF stops the authenticator chain on the first raise, so a valid session/JWT request carrying ordinary headers (no API key) gets 401 before the next authenticator runs.

### Stack

Django + DRF (the raise vs return None contract is DRF-specific; the credentials-from-open-ended-headers trap generalizes)

### Failure mode

Two idiom traps in a custom authenticator: (1) return None means 'not my scheme, try the next' while raise means 'stop, reject' — raising on 'couldn't parse my creds' kills the fallback to session/JWT; (2) parsing creds from an unconstrained header set makes the auth surface every header and order-dependent, plus a non-constant-time secret compare is a timing side channel.

### Evidence

```python
class MachineApiKeyAuth(BaseAuthentication):
    def authenticate(self, request):
        username = secret = None
        for k, v in request.headers.items():  # scans ALL headers
            if k.lower() in KNOWN_STANDARD_HEADERS: continue
            username, secret = k, v; break
        if not username:
            raise AuthenticationFailed("no creds")  # BUG: should return None
        if token.secret_key != secret:  # BUG: not constant-time
            raise AuthenticationFailed("bad key")
```

### Verifier idea

1) A valid session cookie + ordinary headers and NO API key must authenticate (proves the authenticator returned None, didn't raise). 2) A junk header must not trigger a user DB lookup. 3) Assert hmac.compare_digest on the secret path.

### Best target artifact

New failure card

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.

title: [case] Method-based authZ 'fixed' by nulling the permission flag for read-over-POST actions
body:
### Summary

A permission mixin decides read vs write by HTTP method: safe methods get a light check, everything else requires a write permission flag. Two operations that are semantically reads (search-by-key, bulk-get) are exposed over POST, so they land in the write branch. Instead of classifying the operation, the code sets permission_flag = None for those actions — removing authorization rather than correcting it. Any authenticated principal can now run them.

### Stack

Django + DRF (method-based authorization exists in many frameworks)

### Failure mode

Method-based authZ misclassifies read-over-POST / write-over-GET; the remediation disables the guard (flag = None) instead of mapping action to required permission. The bypass is invisible because it looks like an intentional 'this action is a read' comment.

### Evidence

```python
class ImageLinkViewSet(PermissionFlagMixin, ModelViewSet):
    permission_flag = "can_moderate"
    def check_permissions(self, request):
        if self.action in {"by_key", "bulk_get"}:  # reads over POST
            self.permission_flag = None  # authZ removed, not fixed
            super().check_permissions(request)
            self.permission_flag = "can_moderate"
            return
        super().check_permissions(request)
```

### Verifier idea

A principal without the read permission must get 403 on POST .../by-key/. Safe pattern: an explicit action-to-required-permission map (per-action authorization), not method inference and not nulling the flag.

### Best target artifact

New failure card

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.

title: [case] No default throttle; token/login endpoint unthrottled (credential brute force)
body:
### Summary

No global rate limiting is configured; a single endpoint has an ad-hoc throttle, the rest have none. The token/login endpoint accepts unlimited attempts → credential brute force; heavy list/bulk endpoints have no per-caller cost ceiling.

### Stack

Django + DRF (applies to any API without gateway-level rate limiting)

### Failure mode

Unrestricted resource consumption (OWASP API4) plus brute-forceable auth (no throttle on the token endpoint). Compounds with a custom authenticator that does a DB user lookup per request — a cheap request amplifies into DB load.

### Evidence

```python
REST_FRAMEWORK = {  # no DEFAULT_THROTTLE_CLASSES / DEFAULT_THROTTLE_RATES
    "DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES": (...),
}
urlpatterns = [path("api/token/", TokenObtainPairView.as_view())]  # no dedicated throttle
```

### Verifier idea

Test: N+1 rapid requests to the token endpoint return 429; assert a dedicated stricter throttle scope on the auth/login route distinct from the general API rate.

### Best target artifact

Checker

### Privacy check

- [x] I removed company names, credentials, PII, private URLs, and customer data.
- [x] I checked existing failure cards for duplicates.
